text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Patel 
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr A. Patel 
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K732914 NHS No. 9076314528  
12 Brookfield Road, London, SE15 3QT

Thank you for asking me to review your patient, whom I saw today in the neurology clinic with his partner present. He has a confirmed diagnosis of focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset by prior semiology and EEG correlation. He reports brief focal aware episodes consisting of sudden epigastric rising sensation with déjà vu and a metallic taste, lasting 30–60 seconds, occasionally followed by impaired awareness and automatisms. There has been no evolution to generalised tonic–clonic activity in the past two years.

On current treatment with lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and lacosamide 100 mg twice daily, his seizure pattern has remained steady, with focal aware events occurring approximately once every 6–8 weeks and rare focal impaired awareness events every 3–4 months, typically precipitated by missed sleep or intercurrent illness. He and his partner feel the current balance of efficacy and tolerability is acceptable. In this context, I would characterise his seizure control as remaining steady over recent months, with no discernible escalation in frequency or severity. There have been no injuries, emergency presentations, or postictal confusion lasting beyond one hour.

He denies nocturnal seizures, tongue biting, or incontinence. There is no current mood disturbance, psychosis, or significant anxiety reported, though he notes mild anticipatory worry around high-stress work deadlines, which he manages with routine and sleep hygiene. No adverse effects from medication apart from occasional mild dizziness when standing quickly; no rash, diplopia, or ataxia.

Neurological examination today was unremarkable. Cardiovascular observations were normal. Recent bloods (U&Es, LFTs, FBC) from his GP last month were within reference ranges. A prior MRI brain (epilepsy protocol, 2023) showed subtle right mesial temporal T2/FLAIR signal consistent with mesial temporal sclerosis. A 48-hour ambulatory EEG in 2023 demonstrated right temporal interictal epileptiform discharges without captured clinical events.

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine and lacosamide at current doses given the maintained pattern of events and acceptable tolerability.
- Reinforce adherence and sleep hygiene; patient is already using phone reminders to avoid missed doses.
- Rescue plan: should clusters occur (>2 focal impaired awareness events in 24 hours) or any generalised events recur, he will contact our epilepsy nurse specialist for expedited review.
- Routine safety counselling reviewed (driving regulations, bathing precautions, work-at-height avoidance); he confirms compliance and is not currently driving.
- Follow-up in 9–12 months, or sooner if there is any increase in frequency, change in semiology, or emergence of generalised seizures.

Please let me know if you would like any further information. I will copy this letter to the patient and to the epilepsy nurse specialist.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: focal aware events occurring approximately once every 6–8 weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states two seizure frequencies: focal aware events occur approximately once every 6–8 weeks, and focal impaired awareness events occur every 3–4 months. Per the rule to select the highest frequency, we choose the more frequent pattern (once every 6–8 weeks). This is not seizure-free, so we report the calculated frequency. Convert to allowed format: 1 per 6 to 8 week.
seizure_frequency_number: focal aware events occurring approximately once every 6–8 weeks

flaws.description: Gold (1 per 6 to 8 week) disagrees with Reference (seizure free for multiple month)
